findings.finding: server.js eagerly imports 156 route modules and mounts 176 `app.use('/api/...')` handlers in a single 982-line file (7 routes are dynamically imported, the rest static). On Vercel the whole app loads as one serverless function (api/index.js → server.js).
findings.consequence: Every cold start pays the import cost of all 384 services transitively reachable from 176 routers, even for a request that touches one endpoint. The single mounting file is also a constant merge-conflict point and makes it hard to see the API surface. Not a correctness bug, but a cold-start and maintainability tax that grows with each feature.
findings.recommendation: Group routers into feature index modules (routes/index.auth.js, routes/index.twin.js, ...) and mount those, shrinking server.js. Consider lazy/dynamic import for low-traffic route groups (crons, admin, test-*) to cut cold-start work. Drop the test-*/debug-* routes from the production build.

findings.title: Two directories with identically-named extractor files for different purposes (collision-by-name)
findings.finding: `api/services/extractors/` (calendarExtractor.js, discordExtractor.js, githubExtractor.js, spotifyExtractor.js) pulls raw platform data, while `api/services/featureExtractors/` (same four names plus more) derives Big-Five personality features. They are genuinely different concerns and both are live (extractors/ imported by soul-extraction.js; featureExtractors/ imported by nango.js + tests), but share filenames (e.g. two `calendarExtractor.js` exporting `CalendarExtractor` vs `CalendarFeatureExtractor`).
findings.consequence: High cognitive load and import-mistake risk: a developer can import the wrong `calendarExtractor.js` and get a class with a completely different contract. Grep/IDE jump-to-file lands on the wrong one. Not a bug today, but an easy future one.
findings.recommendation: Rename files to encode purpose (e.g. `extractors/calendarDataExtractor.js`, `featureExtractors/calendarFeatureExtractor.js`) or merge the two directories under a clear `raw/` vs `features/` split so filenames are unambiguous.

findings.line: transactions.js:250; ~270 bare error responses across api/routes/*.js
findings.title: ~1/3 of error responses omit the `success` flag, deviating from the house envelope; one path leaks raw DB error
findings.finding: Counting JSON error responses across api/routes/*.js: ~470 use `{ success: ... }` while ~270 return bare `{ error: ... }` with no `success` flag, contradicting the documented envelope `{ success, data?, error?, meta? }` (patterns.md: 'success flag always present'). Separately, transactions.js:250 returns `{ success:false, error:'Failed to save transactions', detail: insertErr.message }` — leaking the raw DB error message unconditionally, even though the same file defines a prod-safe `safeError()` helper (lines 33-37) used everywhere else.
findings.consequence: Frontend clients must handle two error shapes (presence/absence of `success`), making generic error handling brittle. The transactions.js:250 leak exposes internal DB error text to the client in production.
findings.recommendation: Standardize error responses on the `{ success:false, error }` envelope (a shared error helper or the existing errorHandler middleware). Route transactions.js:250 through the existing `safeError()` helper so the DB message is suppressed in production.

findings.title: Repeated 'ignore duplicate edge' swallowed-catch block copied 7 times
findings.finding: brainMigrationService.js repeats the same try/catch-and-ignore pattern for edge insertion at 7 sites (e.g. 261-263 '// Ignore duplicate edge errors', 534-536 '// Ignore if already connected', 557-559 '// Ignore'). Each silently swallows the error with only a comment. One (557-559) has the bare comment '// Ignore' with no justification of which error class is expected.
findings.consequence: If the underlying insert starts failing for a non-duplicate reason (constraint change, RLS denial, connection error), all 7 sites silently drop it, masking real failures during a migration that is supposed to be one-shot/durable. The duplication also means a fix (e.g. checking the error code) must be applied 7 times.
findings.recommendation: Extract a single `safeInsertEdge(...)` helper that inspects the error and only swallows the known duplicate/unique-violation code (e.g. Postgres 23505), logging anything else at warn. Replace the 7 inline catches with calls to it.

findings.title: claude-sync reads ~/.claude from the local filesystem — effectively a no-op on Vercel serverless
findings.finding: `extractConversationsFromProjects` (177+) and the `/run` handler (291+) walk `os.homedir()/.claude/projects` via `fs.readdirSync`/`fs.readFileSync` to ingest Claude Code conversation logs. On Vercel serverless the deploy container's homedir has no `.claude` directory, so `fs.existsSync` returns false and the route always returns empty (181-184). The path is derived from `os.homedir()` (lines 24-28), not user input, so there is no traversal risk.
findings.consequence: Auth-gated and harmless, but it is production-inert code that looks functional — a maintenance trap (someone may debug 'why does claude-sync return nothing' in prod). Adds surface area and import weight for no production value.
findings.recommendation: Confirm whether this route is intended only for local/desktop use; if so, gate it behind an explicit env flag (or remove from the Vercel deploy) and document that it is local-only. If it is meant to work in prod, the data source needs to be uploaded by the client, not read from the server filesystem.

findings.title: Several single-function parsers exceed 300-450 lines inside the 4057-line module
findings.finding: gdprImportService.js is well-decomposed into ~60 per-platform parser functions and a clean `processGdprImport` switch dispatch (3944-4010) with a throwing default — but individual parsers are very long: `parseAppleHealth` ~450 lines (1133-1582) and `parseSpotifyExtended` ~307 lines (171-478). The file as a whole is 4057 lines, well past the ~800-line smell threshold, though it is cohesive (one parser registry).
findings.consequence: The longest parsers are hard to unit-test in isolation and hard to review; a regression in one record-type branch of parseAppleHealth is easy to miss. Low frequency (import path), so blast radius is limited, but coverage of the tricky parsing paths is exactly where this house values tests.
findings.recommendation: Split the largest parsers (Apple Health, Spotify Extended) into sub-parsers by record type and add focused unit tests for each (pure functions, easy to test). Optionally split the module into a `parsers/` directory, one file per platform, re-exported by an index.

findings.title: decryptState failure swallowed at info level without the error object
findings.finding: Both OAuth callback handlers wrap `decryptState(state)` in try/catch that only logs `log.info('Could not decode state ...')` and discards the caught error `e` (1212-1214 and 1442-1444). A state that fails to decrypt could indicate tampering or a key-rotation/version mismatch, not just an absent state.
findings.consequence: A decrypt failure (potential CSRF-state tampering or a deployment key mismatch) is logged as routine info with no error detail, so it will not stand out in logs or alerting. The flow then proceeds with `provider='google'` defaults, which may mask the real cause of failed connector logins.
findings.recommendation: Log decrypt failures at `warn` with the error message (not the state value) so tampering/key-mismatch is observable; consider rejecting outright when `state` was present but failed to decrypt in an auth (non-connector) flow.

findings.title: formatRelativeTime copy-pasted (byte-identical) across 5+ components
findings.finding: formatRelativeTime is independently defined in EmailTriageCard.tsx:14, RelationshipsCard.tsx:16 (byte-for-byte identical to EmailTriageCard), ConversationList.tsx:20, WorkspaceActionCard.tsx:48, ProactiveInsightsPanel.tsx:47, MessageList.tsx:46, and spotifyTypes.ts:88 — plus closely-related variants formatTimeAgo (WeeklySynthesisCard.tsx:34, TwinReflection.tsx:27) and formatTimelineDate (dataTransformers.ts:192). Several differ subtly (some take Date, some string; some say 'yesterday', some don't), so they are not even consistent.
findings.consequence: Inconsistent relative-time copy across the UI ('yesterday' vs '1d ago'), and any fix to the formatting logic must be applied in 7+ places. Pure maintenance tax.
findings.recommendation: Create src/utils/formatRelativeTime.ts accepting (Date | string) and import it everywhere; delete the local copies. Reconcile the 'yesterday' vs 'Nd ago' wording while consolidating.

findings.title: Many fire-and-forget fetches swallow errors with empty .catch(() => {})
findings.finding: There are ~24 empty .catch(() => {}) / catch {} sites across the frontend, clustered in Settings.tsx (lines 115,151,269,274,281,286), AuthContext.tsx (272,280,317,340), and various dashboard cards (DailyTimeline.tsx:45,55; HeroInsight.tsx:21; ProactiveInsightsPanel.tsx:82). Several have justifying comments (apiBase, useWeather permissions), but the Settings.tsx ones do not — they silently drop failures of user-initiated settings mutations.
findings.consequence: When a settings save or a side-effecting POST fails, the user gets no feedback and the developer gets no log — directly violating the project's 'errors are never silent' rule. A user can believe a preference was saved when it was not.
findings.recommendation: For genuinely fire-and-forget telemetry/prefetch calls, add a one-line comment justifying the swallow (the house rule). For user-initiated mutations in Settings.tsx, surface a toast on failure (the codebase already has useToast/ErrorContext).

findings.title: Public routes (/s/:userId, /p/:userId, /download, /privacy-policy, /terms) render outside SidebarLayout's ErrorBoundary granularity but a few public pages lack their own boundary
findings.finding: Authenticated routes consistently wrap their page in a route-level <ErrorBoundary> inside SidebarLayout (good). The public portfolio routes do wrap PortfolioPage in ErrorBoundary (App.tsx:427-438), but DownloadPage (line 547), PrivacyPolicy (550), and TermsOfService (553) are rendered with no route-level ErrorBoundary — they rely solely on the single top-level boundary at App.tsx:132, which on error replaces the ENTIRE app shell with the full-screen fallback.
findings.consequence: A render error in any of these public pages blanks the whole application to the generic error screen instead of isolating to the page, degrading the first-impression surfaces (download/legal pages that unauthenticated visitors hit).
findings.recommendation: Wrap the remaining public page elements (DownloadPage, PrivacyPolicy, TermsOfService, and the eager OAuthCallback/DesktopHandoff) in route-level ErrorBoundary, consistent with the authenticated routes.

findings.title: Untyped any on API response mapping and caught errors in admin/eval pages
findings.finding: EvalDashboard.tsx uses catch (err: any) at line 142 and untyped (r: any)/(run: any) callbacks at lines 215, 218, 433 when mapping API response rows. Similar untyped Record<string, any> / data?: any appear in the chat ActionEvent params/data (TalkToTwin.tsx:27,29; WorkspaceActionCard.tsx:10,12; MessageList.tsx:59,61). Note: dataTransformers.ts (9 anys) is NOT a problem — each is explicitly annotated with an eslint-disable + justification for genuinely dynamic API shapes; that is the right pattern.
findings.consequence: Response-shape changes from the backend won't be caught for these mapped rows; a renamed field surfaces as undefined at runtime. Combined with strictNullChecks:false, these any-typed mappings are blind spots.
findings.recommendation: Define minimal response interfaces for the eval/admin endpoints and the SSE action payloads, and type the .map/.filter callbacks against them. Follow the dataTransformers pattern (eslint-disable + reason) only where the shape is truly dynamic.

findings.title: Hardcoded HMAC fallback secret for channel-ownership OTPs (CWE-798)
findings.finding: hashCode() signs the OTP HMAC with `process.env.OTP_HMAC_SECRET || process.env.JWT_SECRET || 'twinme-otp-fallback'`. If both env vars were unset, OTP codes would be HMAC'd with a secret hardcoded in public source. In practice JWT_SECRET is required at boot (api/routes/auth-simple.js:177 throws if missing), so the literal fallback is effectively dead — but it is a latent footgun. Separately, deriving the OTP HMAC key from JWT_SECRET couples two unrelated secrets (rotating JWT_SECRET silently invalidates all in-flight OTPs).
findings.consequence: If a future refactor or a non-API entrypoint (script, worker) loads this module without JWT_SECRET set, OTPs verifying WhatsApp/Telegram channel ownership would be forgeable by anyone reading the source, allowing an attacker to link a victim's phone/channel to their own account. Low likelihood given the current boot guard, hence Low severity.
findings.recommendation: Remove the 'twinme-otp-fallback' literal and fail closed (throw) when neither OTP_HMAC_SECRET nor JWT_SECRET is present, mirroring the JWT_SECRET boot guard. Prefer a dedicated OTP_HMAC_SECRET so OTP integrity is not coupled to JWT rotation. Same dead-fallback note applies softly to api/services/llmGateway.js:236 (cost/availability, not auth).

findings.title: SKIP_CRON_AUTH env var can disable all cron authentication outside production
findings.finding: verifyCronSecret returns `{ authorized: true }` unconditionally when `SKIP_CRON_AUTH === 'true' && NODE_ENV !== 'production'`. The production guard is correct (it won't fire when NODE_ENV=production), and CLAUDE.md warns never to set it in prod. But any preview/staging deploy where NODE_ENV is not exactly the string 'production' (Vercel preview deployments default to a non-production environment) would have ALL cron endpoints (memory mutation, LLM-spend jobs, data deletion) fully open if this flag is ever set. There is no audit log when the bypass activates.
findings.consequence: A network-reachable preview/staging environment with SKIP_CRON_AUTH=true exposes every cron route (e.g. memory-forgetting soft-deletes, observation ingestion, deliver-insights) to unauthenticated invocation, enabling data tampering and uncapped LLM cost amplification against that environment.
findings.recommendation: Tighten the guard to require an explicit dev-only flag AND log a WARN every time the bypass is taken so it is visible in deploy logs. Consider keying off a positive allowlist (e.g. only when NODE_ENV==='development' AND not on Vercel) rather than the negative `!== 'production'` check, since Vercel preview NODE_ENV is not 'production'.

findings.title: Dev-gated internal detail leakage relies entirely on NODE_ENV being exactly 'production'
findings.finding: authenticateUser returns `tokenExpired`/`details` (verifyError.message/name) and the unexpected-error handler returns `error.message` only when NODE_ENV === 'development'. errorHandler.js applies the same NODE_ENV gate for stack traces and DB error messages (errorHandler.js:123-131, 181-188). This is the correct pattern, but the entire information-disclosure posture hinges on NODE_ENV being set to exactly the string 'production' in every deployed environment. Vercel preview deployments and any misconfigured env where NODE_ENV is unset/blank/'preview' would leak JWT verification internals, stack traces, and raw DB error messages to clients.
findings.consequence: In a preview or misconfigured environment, error responses echo stack traces, jsonwebtoken error details, and database error messages — aiding attackers in fingerprinting the stack and crafting targeted attacks. Bounded to non-production envs, so Low.
findings.recommendation: Gate verbose errors on an explicit affirmative flag (e.g. DEBUG_ERRORS==='true') rather than `NODE_ENV==='development'`, and assert NODE_ENV is one of a known set at boot. This makes 'leak internals' opt-in rather than the default for any environment that isn't literally 'production'.

findings.title: redact() masks only by field name; high-PII fields like content, body, text, message, query are not in PII_FIELDS
findings.finding: redact() (33-52) replaces values only when the key is in PII_FIELDS = {email, phone, token, password, secret, authorization, access_token, refresh_token, api_key...}. Free-text fields that routinely carry the most sensitive content — memory content, message body, chat text, search query, name, address — are NOT redacted. The protection is therefore entirely dependent on every caller never passing those fields. Sampling current call sites (memoryStreamService.js, whatsappInboundPipeline.js, twin-chat.js) shows callers are presently careful (only metadata like messageId and last-4 phone digits are logged), so this is a latent defense-in-depth gap, not an active leak.
findings.consequence: A future log statement that includes log.info('saved', { content }) or { message } would silently write the user's private twin data / chat content to Vercel logs and Sentry in cleartext, because the redactor would not catch it. The guarantee implied by an auto-redacting logger is weaker than it appears.
findings.recommendation: Add content, body, text, message, query, name, address to PII_FIELDS (truncate or hash rather than full-redact if some prefix is needed for debugging), or add a value-based heuristic (redact strings over N chars / matching email/phone regex). Document that free-text must be passed under a recognized key.

findings.title: Untracked Playwright accessibility-snapshot YAMLs left in repo root
findings.finding: Three untracked files (iniciador-dropdown.yml, iniciador-step-final.yml, iniciador-step-next.yml) in the repo root are Playwright accessibility-tree snapshots of a third-party (Iniciador) onboarding iframe. They contain no secrets in the heads I inspected, but they are stray test artifacts at the project root that could be accidentally committed and may capture form field values / business answers from a live session.
findings.consequence: Low: risk of committing scratch artifacts (form contents, internal answers) into version history, and root-level clutter. Not a code-execution or auth risk.
findings.recommendation: Delete the scratch files or move them under a tests/fixtures path and add a .gitignore rule for *.yml snapshots at root. Confirm they contain no captured PII before any commit.

findings.title: Self-learned directives are injected as binding high-attention rules with only length truncation, no content validation
findings.finding: The DIRECTIVES YOU'VE LEARNED block injects twin_directives rows as '...durable rules learned from past corrections. Follow them exactly:' high in the prompt (above temporal context) so the model treats them as binding. The only defense applied is per-directive length truncation to 150 chars (307-309). Directives originate from automated extraction of past user corrections and can be user-edited up to 2000 chars (per the inline comment). If the extraction step ever ingests injected content (e.g. a correction the user was tricked into making, or attacker-influenced conversation text fed to the extractor), a malicious 'rule' becomes a persistent, high-priority instruction across all future sessions.
findings.consequence: A one-time successful injection or a poisoned correction could persist as a durable behavioral override for the twin, surviving across conversations — a more serious outcome than a single-turn hijack because it is sticky.
findings.recommendation: Validate/scan directive content at write time (reject directives containing instruction-like control phrases or role markers), and frame the injected block defensively (these are user preferences about tone/behavior, not security-relevant commands; they never override safety or grounding rules). Keep the strongest weight reserved for the static base instructions.

findings.title: Markdown rendering is XSS-safe today but lacks an explicit skipHtml guard against future rehype-raw addition
findings.finding: Twin output is rendered via react-markdown with remarkPlugins=[remarkGfm] only (MessageList.tsx:158-160, FeaturedMemory.tsx:111). No rehype-raw plugin is present, so raw HTML in model/LLM output is NOT rendered as HTML by default — this is currently safe (verified: zero dangerouslySetInnerHTML uses across src/). The residual risk is purely forward-looking: there is no explicit skipHtml prop documenting the invariant, so a future contributor adding rehype-raw (commonly added to 'fix' HTML passthrough) would silently turn LLM output — which can be steered by the prompt-injection surface above — into an XSS vector.
findings.consequence: No current vulnerability. If rehype-raw were later added without review, attacker-influenced twin output could execute script in the user's session. Flagging to preserve the safe default.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit skipHtml prop (or a code comment asserting 'do not add rehype-raw — LLM output is untrusted') to the ReactMarkdown call sites to make the invariant intentional and review-visible.

findings.title: OTP cooldown test relies on real wall-clock timing (mild flake risk)
findings.finding: The OTP test asserts security behavior well (hashed-not-plaintext at :64-65, single-use, attempt cap, expiry-before-hash-compare at :119-123) — strong coverage. But the cooldown case (:80-87) constructs a created_at of `Date.now() - 5*1000` against the service's real cooldown window and asserts success===false with retryAfterMs>0, using real Date.now() rather than vi.useFakeTimers. The expiry helpers (:44-45) are also wall-clock based.
findings.consequence: If the service's resend-cooldown window is ever tuned close to 5s, or under a slow/loaded CI runner the 5s-ago timestamp drifts relative to the cooldown boundary, the cooldown test could flake. Low likelihood today (the margin looks comfortable) but it is the kind of time-coupled assertion the CI already battles elsewhere.
findings.recommendation: Use vi.useFakeTimers()/vi.setSystemTime to make the cooldown boundary deterministic, as 12 other suites already do (e.g. whoop/dateUtils.test.js). Keeps the otherwise-excellent OTP suite immune to runner timing.

findings.title: Whole-describe skips silently remove coverage for shipped features
findings.finding: Beyond the 3 documented memory-retrieval skips, an entire suite is disabled: describe.skip('workspaceActionParser') at tests/api/services/workspaceActionParser.test.js:7 (workspace actions are a live WhatsApp+web feature per CLAUDE.md and task list #74), and a large block of personality prompt-builder assertions is skipped in tests/api/unit/personality.test.js (:32, :167-245, covering deriveSamplingParams and per-OCEAN prompt instructions). 13 it.skip/describe.skip markers total across the suite.
findings.consequence: workspaceActionParser parses untrusted user text into actions — a parser whose only test file is fully skipped means a parsing regression (or an injection-adjacent edge case) ships unguarded. Skipped suites give a false sense of coverage when scanning the tests/ tree.
findings.recommendation: For workspaceActionParser, either delete the stale suite or update it to the current parser shape and unskip — a security-relevant parser should not have an all-skipped test file. Audit the 13 skips and convert each to either a passing test or an explicit removal so the tree reflects real coverage.

findings.title: Soul scoring is well-covered; minor gap on rounding/clamp edges
findings.finding: computeSoulScore is genuinely behavior-tested (tests/unit/soulScoring.test.ts): the 95-cap on stale/expired platforms (:43-46), active-not-total numerator (:32-41), linear memory scaling to 1000 (:53-57), the legacy axesCount no-op (:21-30), and the deprecated deriveAxesCount returning 0 (:60-67). This is a model for the rest of the suite. Gap: no test for memoryCount above 1000 (does the component clamp at 100/3?) or for negative/NaN inputs, and computeDomainScore only has the connected/not-connected binary.
findings.consequence: Low — the load-bearing branches are covered. An out-of-range memoryCount that isn't clamped would over-weight the memory component, but the linear-to-1000 test implies a clamp exists; it just isn't pinned.
findings.recommendation: Add one case for memoryCount=5000 asserting the memory component does not exceed its 100/3 share, and one NaN/negative-guard case. Otherwise leave as-is — this file is a good template.

findings.title: strengthenCoCitedLinks issues up to ~30 serial DB round-trips per call (SELECT-then-write per pair)
findings.finding: For each co-citation pair (capped at 15), the function awaits a SELECT (maybeSingle) then a separate UPDATE or UPSERT, strictly sequentially in `for (const [sourceId, targetId] of pairs)`. That is up to 30 serial Supabase round-trips per call. Called fire-and-forget after each twin chat (twinChatPipeline.js:161).
findings.consequence: Bounded (max 15 pairs) and off the user's response critical path (fire-and-forget), so user-visible latency is unaffected — but it still consumes DB connection time and serverless CPU for ~30 sequential network hops on every chat turn.
findings.recommendation: Replace the SELECT-then-write loop with a single batched upsert using an `ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE SET strength = LEAST(1.0, memory_links.strength + 0.1)` RPC, collapsing ~30 round-trips into one. Pairs are already accumulated in an array.

findings.area: Twin chat critical path / platform analytics
findings.title: Per-chat platform-analytics escalation loops platforms sequentially (token fetch + analytics + learn awaited in series)
findings.finding: When a user message triggers analytics intent, the loop over PLATFORM_ANALYTICS awaits getValidAccessToken (possible token-refresh network call), then the analytics call (Promise.race with timeout), then an inline learn() hook — all sequentially per matching platform. In practice cfg.detect (sync) makes most platforms `continue` cheaply and usually only one platform's intent matches per message, so the serial cost is normally a single platform's chain.
findings.consequence: On the rare message that matches multiple platforms' analytics intent, their chains run back-to-back on the user's chat critical path instead of concurrently, adding their latencies (each bounded by PLATFORM_ANALYTICS_TIMEOUT_MS) to time-to-first-token.
findings.recommendation: Pre-filter to platforms whose intent matched, then run their token+analytics+learn chains via Promise.allSettled so multi-platform turns parallelize. Single-platform turns are unaffected.

findings.title: xlsx CDN pin is correctly handled — kept as a STRENGTH/verification, not a defect
findings.finding: xlsx is intentionally NOT from the npm registry: package.json points to https://cdn.sheetjs.com/xlsx-0.20.3/xlsx-0.20.3.tgz. The lockfile records this URL plus an sha512 integrity hash (package-lock.json:18111-), so `npm ci` stays deterministic. Commit fa3edf6a ("fix(deps): patch xlsx CVEs") shows this was a deliberate move off the registry's frozen 0.18.5 (which carries unpatched prototype-pollution GHSA-4r6h-8v6p-xvw6 + ReDoS GHSA-5pgg-2g8v-p4x9) to the patched SheetJS 0.20.3 build, and added a round-trip parser regression test. npm audit confirms both xlsx HIGH advisories are now cleared (9→8 vulns).
findings.consequence: This is the correct way to handle SheetJS's well-known refusal to publish fixes to npm. The one residual concern is supply-chain: the dependency now bypasses the npm registry's provenance and resolves from cdn.sheetjs.com, so integrity rests entirely on the lockfile hash — anyone who edits package.json without regenerating the lockfile loses verification.
findings.recommendation: No action needed beyond a guard: keep the integrity hash sacrosanct (never hand-edit the xlsx version in package.json without `npm install` regenerating the lockfile hash), and consider a CI check that the xlsx tarball URL + integrity match an approved value. Document the CDN-pin rationale near the dependency so a future dev does not 'helpfully' move it back to the registry.

findings.title: ~50 packages outdated (many multiple majors behind), broad runtime surface (1300 total deps)
findings.finding: npm outdated lists ~50 packages behind latest. Notable major-version gaps that are NOT security issues but accrue maintenance drift: react/react-dom 18.3.1 vs 19.x, @supabase/supabase-js 2.57.4 vs 2.108.2 (50+ minors behind on a security-sensitive client), @elevenlabs/client 0.15→1.12, openai 5.22→6.44, stripe 20.4→22.2 (payments SDK two majors behind), recharts 2.15→3.8, react-router-dom 6.30→7.18, googleapis 161→173, zod 3.25→4.4. npm audit reports 1300 total resolved dependencies (911 prod). Within the declared ranges, even minor catch-up bumps (e.g. @supabase, @tanstack/react-query, framer-motion, ioredis) are not being pulled because the lockfile is pinned older than "Wanted".
findings.consequence: None of these are flagged CVEs today, so severity is Low. The risk is cumulative: a payments SDK (stripe) and DB client (supabase-js) drifting far behind latest means security patches and API deprecations pile up, making the eventual upgrade riskier and a future CVE harder to patch quickly. The large transitive surface (1300 deps) also widens future exposure.
findings.recommendation: Schedule a routine `npm update` to pull in-range minors (clears the gap between Current and Wanted with low risk), prioritizing security-sensitive clients first: @supabase/supabase-js, stripe, ioredis, @sentry/*. Triage the major-version gaps (react 19, stripe 22, supabase deprecations) as separate planned upgrades, not all at once.

findings.title: legacy-peer-deps=true globally suppresses peer-dependency conflict detection
findings.finding: .npmrc contains a single line: `legacy-peer-deps=true`. This makes npm ignore peer-dependency mismatches across the entire install (the npm 6 behavior). The repo is on React 18 with a large Radix UI + framer-motion + @tanstack ecosystem where peer ranges matter.
findings.consequence: Peer-dep conflicts that would normally surface as install errors are silently swallowed, so an incompatible combination (e.g. a Radix component or react-day-picker expecting React 19 peers) can install cleanly yet break at runtime. It also hides the signal that would warn before a risky major upgrade. This is a hygiene/footgun issue, not an active vulnerability.
findings.recommendation: Identify which specific package(s) actually require legacy-peer-deps (likely one or two) and pin the workaround narrowly via an `overrides` entry instead of disabling peer checks repo-wide. If nothing currently needs it, remove the flag and let npm surface real conflicts.

findings.title: No code formatter (Prettier) configured or enforced
findings.finding: There is a `lint` script (package.json:17) but no `format` script and no Prettier/dprint config in the repo root (.prettierrc* / prettier.config.* absent; verified). ESLint is configured for correctness rules only, not formatting. Style consistency across 425 TS/TSX + 592 JS files relies entirely on individual editor settings.
findings.consequence: Diffs accumulate incidental whitespace/quote/semicolon churn, making code review noisier and increasing merge-conflict surface. New contributors have no canonical style, and the absence shows up as inconsistent formatting across files (a known weakness already noted in the frontend review: 'inconsistent component patterns').
findings.recommendation: Add Prettier with a minimal .prettierrc, a `format` and `format:check` script, and run `format:check` as a non-blocking CI step (or wire it into the existing PostToolUse hook the team's rules already describe). Low effort, high consistency payoff.

findings.title: Working tree is cluttered with ~100 root-level debug scripts, screenshots, and one-off test files
findings.finding: The repo root contains dozens of tracked-or-untracked artifacts: PNG/JPEG screenshots (slide1-hero.png, download-live.png, etc.), one-off scripts (test-*.js, fix_app.py, write_audit_spec.py, _write_dashboard.js, theme-diagnostic.mjs), and stray .yml files (iniciador-*.yml — currently untracked per git status). The .gitignore tries to suppress these post-hoc with broad rules like `test-*.js` (line 93) and `*.png` (line 143), but many predate the rules and the patterns are reactive whack-a-mole (the file has dated cleanup comments at lines 214-216).
findings.consequence: A new developer cloning/listing the repo root sees a wall of debug detritus and cannot tell signal from noise — which file is an entry point vs a throwaway probe. It also raises the risk of accidentally committing a secret-bearing scratch script (the .gitignore already calls out 'Test files with secrets' at lines 13-23, evidence this has been a real problem).
findings.recommendation: Move active diagnostic scripts into scripts/ (already conventioned for spikes via the `scripts/_*` ignore) and delete dead screenshots/one-offs. Keep the root to config + entry points. This is housekeeping, not architecture — batch it in a single 'repo hygiene' PR.

findings.title: Integration tests (real Supabase round-trips) never run in CI and are gated only by a glob exclusion
findings.finding: CI runs vitest with `--exclude '**/*.integration.test.js'` (ci.yml:68) because those tests hit the real prod Supabase project (comment lines 52-56). There is exactly 1 *.integration.test.js file (counted) and no scheduled or manual workflow that ever runs it — it is excluded everywhere with no compensating job. The exclusion relies on a naming convention; a future integration test authored without the `.integration.` infix would accidentally run against prod in CI.
findings.consequence: The DB-contract layer (RPC signatures, RLS behavior, migration drift) is never exercised by automation — regressions there surface only in production. Separately, the convention-based guard is brittle: one mis-named test file would fire real prod DB round-trips on every push, a cost and data-integrity risk.
findings.recommendation: Stand up a dedicated, manually/scheduled-triggered job that runs the integration suite against an ephemeral Supabase branch (Supabase supports branch DBs) rather than prod, and harden the guard by making the integration tests opt-IN (a tag or env flag) instead of opt-OUT, so a mis-named file fails closed.

findings.title: Cron inventory in vercel.json (29 crons) has drifted from CLAUDE.md's documented schedule
findings.finding: vercel.json declares 29 cron entries (lines 90-207); the ingest cron is /api/cron/ingest-observations at */30 (lines 92-94) and prospective-check at */15 (lines 116-118). CLAUDE.md's Vercel Cost Rules section names 'deliver-insights and prospective-check at */15' and 'Removed token-refresh cron', but the actual file shows deliver-insights at hourly (0 * * * *, lines 140-142) and a much larger, more granular cron fleet (morning-briefing, inbox-intelligence, relationships, nudge-inactive, department-execute, etc.) than the docs enumerate. The repo map also flagged the cron count as feature-creep ($375 March 2026 incident).
findings.consequence: The cost-critical artifact the team explicitly watches (cron frequency was the documented cause of a $375 overspend) is no longer accurately mirrored in the docs that describe it. An operator auditing 'are we within cost rules?' from CLAUDE.md would get a stale picture and could miss a re-introduced high-frequency cron — exactly the failure mode that caused the original incident.
findings.recommendation: Treat vercel.json as the source of truth and either (a) generate the cron list section of CLAUDE.md from it, or (b) add a CI check that fails if any cron schedule is more frequent than */15 — codifying the cost rule instead of relying on a prose reminder that has already drifted.

findings.title: Dead doc reference: soul-signature voting layer plan .md no longer exists (convention migrated to README.md dirs)
findings.finding: CLAUDE.md:158 points to '.claude/plans/2026-03-08-soul-signature-voting-layer.md (10 tasks, 5 phases)'. That file does not exist; `.claude/plans/` contains only flat dated `.md` files (none for soul-signature) and the doc-file hook now mandates `<date>-<topic>/README.md` dirs (CLAUDE.md:23). The referenced plan was either never created under that name or was removed.
findings.consequence: An engineer wanting the implementation rationale for the personality voting layer follows a dead link. Low impact (the feature is also documented inline in CLAUDE.md), but it's a broken provenance trail.
findings.recommendation: Remove the dead reference or repoint it to the actual plan location if it exists elsewhere.

findings.title: Project-structure tree under-describes migrations: two migration dirs exist, doc names one
findings.finding: CLAUDE.md:346 shows 'database/ # Supabase migrations'. In reality migrations are split across `database/migrations/` (69 files) AND `database/supabase/migrations/` (87 files), plus a lone `api/migrations/001_database_optimization.sql`. The two database/* dirs both hold active migrations (e.g. search_memory_stream RPC and goals tables appear in both trees), with no documented convention for which goes where.
findings.consequence: Engineer adding a migration doesn't know which directory is canonical, risking it being applied in the wrong order or missed. The unexplained split is exactly the kind of undocumented critical behavior that causes schema drift.
findings.recommendation: Document both migration directories and which is authoritative (or consolidate). Note the apply order and the standalone api/migrations file.

findings.title: README describes a different, earlier product than what shipped
findings.finding: Top-level README markets '30+ platforms' including Netflix/TikTok/Twitter (line 5,32,90), a 'Platform Connectors' list claiming LinkedIn/Reddit have APIs (line 43 — contradicts the portfolio cut), a roadmap with 'Blockchain-based identity verification' (line 167), and a trailing '# Automated Deployment Test' fragment (line 183). It omits the entire actual architecture (memory stream, reflections, OpenRouter gateway, WhatsApp/desktop, Vercel crons) that CLAUDE.md documents. README also uses emoji throughout despite the project's hard NO-EMOJIS rule (CLAUDE.md:5).
findings.consequence: The public-facing README is effectively fiction relative to the codebase — a new engineer or external reader gets a wrong product model, and the emoji usage violates a stated project rule in the one doc most likely to be read first.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite README from CLAUDE.md's reality (or make it a thin pointer to CLAUDE.md). Remove the stray deployment-test line, the fictional roadmap items, and emoji to comply with the project rule.

findings.title: Inconsistent gating mechanism documentation for personality reranker (env var vs DB feature flag)
findings.finding: CLAUDE.md:137 says the reranker is 'feature-flagged via ENABLE_PERSONALITY_RERANKER' (an env var), which matches code at `api/services/twinFirstLlmCall.js:120` (`process.env.ENABLE_PERSONALITY_RERANKER === 'true'`). However the broader feature-flag narrative (and the repo map) treats `personality_reranker` as a DB `feature_flags` row alongside llm_wiki/graphRetrieval/neurotransmitter_modes (which ARE DB-backed). The doc never states that reranker is gated differently (env var, not DB flag).
findings.consequence: An operator trying to enable best-of-N reranking via the feature_flags table (the documented mechanism for the other flags) will see no effect, because this one is an env var set at deploy time. Undocumented divergence in the gating model.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly note that personality reranker is gated by the ENABLE_PERSONALITY_RERANKER env var (deploy-time), distinct from the DB-backed feature_flags used by llm_wiki/graphRetrieval/etc.

findings.title: Voice sessions shorter than 4 messages are silently discarded with no feedback
findings.finding: handleVoiceSessionEnd returns early `if (voiceMessages.length < 4) return;` and the underlying hook only fires onSessionEnd when `messagesRef.current.length >= 4` (useVoiceInterview.ts:233). A user who starts voice, says one thing, and the session ends (e.g. mic glitch, quick disconnect) gets nothing saved and no message — the completion pipeline never runs and no error/empty state is shown.
findings.consequence: Short or interrupted voice sessions vanish with zero user feedback; the user may believe their answers were captured when they were not.
findings.recommendation: When a voice session ends below the threshold, surface a brief inline notice ('That was a short one — want to keep going or switch to text?') instead of a silent no-op.

findings.title: Onboarding uses a bespoke amber palette (#E8D5B7 / rgba(232,213,183,*), 158 occurrences) instead of the documented design tokens, and flat non-glass surfaces
findings.finding: Across the onboarding directory the amber color #E8D5B7 / rgba(232,213,183,x) is used 158 times for text, borders, and fills (e.g. ModeSelectionScreen headings/buttons, RevealPhase, DeepInterview caret). The documented system uses --foreground:#F5F5F4 for primary text and a glass recipe of backdrop-blur(42px) + rgba(255,255,255,0.06) bg + rgba(255,255,255,0.10) border for cards. Onboarding cards instead use flat rgba(255,255,255,0.02) fills with no blur (PlatformStep PlatformCard line 23) or blur(20px) (AwakeningScreen GLASS line 57-63), and amber-tinted surfaces.
findings.consequence: Onboarding looks visually disconnected from the rest of the app's dark-glass system; harder to maintain token consistency. Note: no navy backgrounds and no emojis were found, so the hard design rules are respected — this is a consistency deviation, not a rule violation.
findings.recommendation: If the amber 'Ghibli' palette is an intentional onboarding-only theme, codify it as named tokens; otherwise align onboarding surfaces to the standard --foreground text and the blur(42px)/rgba(255,255,255,0.06) glass recipe.

findings.title: 'Question X of 3' counter math is confusing and can show 'Question 1 of 3' for the first two questions
findings.finding: The subheader computes `Question ${Math.max(1, Math.min(questionNumber - 1, 3))} of 3`. Because questionNumber is incremented to nextQ when each assistant message is appended, the displayed index lags the actual question and the Math.max(1,...) clamp means the very first state and the second can both render 'Question 1 of 3'. The ModeSelectionScreen also promises '3 questions. 2 minutes.' while the backend can complete after 2 covered domains (onboarding-calibration.js:602-604), so the real count varies.
findings.consequence: Progress indicator does not reliably reflect where the user is, undermining the sense of a short, bounded interview.
findings.recommendation: Derive the displayed counter from the actual count of user answers (messages.filter(role==='user').length + 1) and reconcile the '3 questions' copy with the adaptive completion logic.

findings.title: handleVoiceSessionEnd references getAuthToken() before its definition (fragile temporal coupling)
findings.finding: getAuthToken is declared as a const arrow function at line 241, but is called inside the handleVoiceSessionEnd useCallback defined at line 103-142 (line 111). It works at runtime only because the callback executes after render (closure over the later-defined const), but it is a TDZ hazard if the call order ever changes, and it's confusing to read.
findings.consequence: No current bug, but fragile: a refactor that invokes the callback synchronously during render, or reorders declarations, would throw a ReferenceError.
findings.recommendation: Move the getAuthToken definition above the callbacks that use it (or inline `getAccessToken()` directly), so declaration precedes use.

findings.title: Cold-start context degradation is silent to the user even when most context is dropped
findings.finding: fetchTwinContext races the whole fan-out against a 10s global breaker and each leg against 6s (lines 275-303, 474-504). On a cold start (Nango token refresh, cold pgbouncer) legs time out and return defaults (memories=[], twinSummary=null, etc.); circuitBreakerTripped is recorded in timings but only logged/persisted — it is never surfaced to the response or the client. The twin then answers from the base prompt alone.
findings.consequence: On the first message of a session (or any slow backend moment), the twin can answer with little/no personal memory yet present a fully confident response, and the user has no signal that it was operating degraded. This is the documented ~25% cold-start memory-miss case. Functionally the chat still returns (good), but quality silently drops with no UX acknowledgement or retry hint.
findings.recommendation: When circuitBreakerTripped is true, surface a soft signal in contextSources (e.g. degraded:true) so the UI can show a subtle 'still warming up — answers get sharper as I load your data' hint, and/or bias the prompt toward the WHEN-UNCERTAIN base rules. No need to fail the turn.

findings.title: Large memory context is appended onto an unrelated trailing block and escapes prompt caching
findings.finding: appendAdditionalContextToPrompt appends the full additional-context string (which includes the retrieved memory stream, up to MAX_ADDITIONAL_CONTEXT_CHARS=12000) onto the LAST system block if that block lacks cache_control (lines 203-205). By the time twin-chat.js calls it (line 346), assembleTwinSystemPrompt has already pushed several trailing blocks (anti-emoji, persona, personality, voice, oracle, financial, neurotransmitter, nudge). So the memory block is concatenated onto whatever small block happened to be last, and only the base instruction block carries cache_control { ephemeral } (twinSystemPromptBuilder.js:663-668).
findings.consequence: Functionally harmless to the LLM, but the largest, most variable context is not isolated for prompt caching and is glued to a semantically unrelated block. This reduces cache-hit efficiency (cost) and makes prompt structure fragile — reordering trailing blocks silently changes where memories land.
findings.recommendation: Push additional context as its own dedicated block rather than mutating the last block's text, and consider explicit cache_control boundaries so the stable prefix (base + identity) is cached separately from the volatile per-message memory block.

findings.title: useIdentityContext flag derived but never consumed by the chat route
findings.finding: deriveFeatureFlagBooleans returns useIdentityContext (twinChatPreFlightChecks.js:30) but twin-chat.js destructures only 7 of the 8 flags (lines 171-174) — useIdentityContext is omitted. identityContext is still fetched unconditionally inside fetchTwinContext (twinContextBuilder.js:407) and injected via buildAdditionalContext regardless of the flag.
findings.consequence: The identity_context feature flag is effectively inert for the chat path: toggling it off in the DB does not actually disable identity-context injection. Minor — it is a no-op flag, not a crash, but it is a misleading control surface for ops.
findings.recommendation: Either wire useIdentityContext through to gate inferIdentityContext / its injection, or remove the flag from deriveFeatureFlagBooleans to avoid implying a control that does nothing.

findings.title: /settings/voice (Voice Bridge) page is orphaned — no UI entry point links to it
findings.finding: VoiceSetupPage is routed at /settings/voice (App.tsx:384) but a search across src/ for 'settings/voice', 'voice-bridge', or navigation to it returns only the route definition and the page's own fetch calls — no button, Link, or navigate() anywhere points users to it. Settings.tsx (the natural parent) has a Messaging section with Telegram + WhatsApp but no link to the voice bridge.
findings.consequence: The whole WhatsApp voice-linking flow is only reachable by manually typing the URL. A built feature is effectively invisible to users.
findings.recommendation: Add an entry point (e.g. a row in Settings → Messaging, or a card) that navigates to /settings/voice, or confirm the page is intentionally retired and remove the route.

findings.title: WhatsAppCaptureCard reads res.json() without checking res.ok
findings.finding: The link-status fetch (lines 420-424) does `const data = await res.json()` with no `res.ok` guard. On a non-2xx response that still returns JSON, it reads data.success/data.linked from an error envelope; on a non-JSON error it throws into the catch (which sets linked:false). Either way it degrades to the 'Link WhatsApp' CTA, so there is no broken UI — but the pattern diverges from the project's res.ok-first convention used elsewhere (e.g. VoiceSetupPage, Settings).
findings.consequence: Minor: a server error is silently treated as 'not linked', so a genuinely linked user could briefly see the 'Link WhatsApp' CTA during an API hiccup. No crash, no data loss.
findings.recommendation: Guard with `if (!res.ok) { setLinked(false); return; }` before parsing, matching the res.ok-first pattern in the rest of the codebase.

findings.title: Unlink uses blocking native confirm() dialog instead of the app's toast/modal pattern
findings.finding: unlink() calls window-level `confirm('Unlink your WhatsApp...')` (line 109). DataExportsPage uses the same window.confirm pattern (line 339) for destructive deletes. The rest of the app uses sonner toasts and in-page confirmation UI (e.g. Settings delete-account uses an inline 'type DELETE' confirm). Native confirm()/alert() are not styleable, break the dark design system, and on some mobile webviews/Tauri can render inconsistently.
findings.consequence: Inconsistent confirmation UX: a jarring OS dialog appears for unlink/export-delete while every other destructive action uses in-app UI. Minor design-system and consistency gap, not a functional break.
findings.recommendation: Replace window.confirm with the app's inline-confirm or a styled modal for consistency; reserve native dialogs for genuine fallbacks.

findings.area: Money page (WhatsApp capture card loading state)
findings.title: WhatsAppCaptureCard shows the 'Link WhatsApp' CTA during initial load (linked === null)
findings.finding: linked is initialized to null and only set after the /whatsapp-link/status fetch resolves. The render (lines 461-487) treats anything non-true as not-linked, so during the in-flight window an already-linked user sees the 'Link WhatsApp' button, which then flips to the 'WhatsApp linked' badge once the fetch returns. There is no loading/skeleton state for this card.
findings.consequence: A linked user briefly sees a 'Link WhatsApp' CTA flash before it corrects — a small content shift / confusing flicker on the Money page header card.
findings.recommendation: While linked === null, render a neutral placeholder (or hide the status chip) instead of the unlinked CTA to avoid the flash.

findings.title: Per-endpoint API errors are logged at warn and swallowed without distinguishing auth failure
findings.finding: After a successful token fetch, Discord's primary guilds call and Calendar's event calls catch all errors with log.warn and continue (returning whatever observations accumulated). Unlike GitHub, neither inspects status for 401/403. For Discord the guilds endpoint is the primary signal — a 401 there yields an empty/partial result classified as no_new_data, not expired. (Lower severity than Whoop because Discord/Calendar tokens are auto-refreshed by getValidAccessToken first, so a post-refresh 401 is rarer.)
findings.consequence: A Discord/Calendar grant revoked at the provider (rare but possible — user removes the app authorization) won't surface a reconnect prompt; the connector silently degrades to producing no observations while reading as healthy.
findings.recommendation: Mirror the GitHub pattern: on the primary endpoint's catch, check e.response?.status === 401/403 and throw a tagged AUTH_FAILED so ingestion marks the connection expired. Secondary endpoints can keep degrading gracefully.

findings.dimension: Security — debug logging of OAuth internals
findings.title: Verbose OAuth diagnostic logging in production paths
findings.finding: refreshAccessToken logs config presence, refresh_token length, client_secret length, and full request param shape on every refresh (lines 145-150, 187-196). connectors.js logs callback code length, redirect URI, available config keys, hasClientSecret on every callback. oauth-callback.js logs code length and redirect URI. None log the secret values themselves, but client_secret length + clientId prefix (entertainment-connectors.js:433) narrows brute-force surface and clutters logs with auth metadata.
findings.consequence: Auth metadata (token lengths, client_id prefixes, secret lengths) lands in Vercel/Sentry logs on every connect/refresh, contrary to the house rule 'error responses/logs don't echo internals.' Low exploitability but a hygiene and compliance issue for an auth surface.
findings.recommendation: Demote these to log.debug (gated off in production) and drop secret-length / client_id-prefix fields entirely. Keep only the structured error (invalid_grant/invalid_client) needed for diagnosis, which is already captured in last_sync_error.

findings.title: Connector proxy uses localhost/PORT defaults that don't match the dev server port
findings.finding: POST /api/connectors/connect/:platform for spotify/youtube/netflix proxies an internal fetch to `${process.env.API_URL || 'http://localhost:'+(process.env.PORT||3001)}/api/entertainment/connect/${platform}`. The documented dev backend port is 3004 (CLAUDE.md), not 3001, and the proxied request forwards no Authorization header — it relies on the entertainment route's authenticateUser, which would reject the unauthenticated internal call. This path also defeats the purpose of having authenticateUser on the outer route by making a second uncredentialed hop.
findings.consequence: If the frontend ever hits this proxy route for Spotify/YouTube (rather than the entertainment route directly), it 401s on the internal hop or connects to the wrong port in dev. It is a latent broken path that duplicates working flows.
findings.recommendation: Remove the internal HTTP self-proxy; call the entertainment connect handler in-process (shared function) or have the frontend call /api/entertainment/connect/:platform directly. If kept, forward the Authorization header and fix the default port.

findings.line: 5-21 (reminders_user_id_fkey uncovered); also twin_corrections, twin_directives, whatsapp_messages, brain_activity_log, pl_raw_behavioral_events, realtime_events
findings.title: 9 foreign keys lack a covering index (advisor: unindexed_foreign_keys)
findings.finding: The live performance advisor lists 9 unindexed_foreign_keys: reminders.user_id, twin_corrections.{conversation_id,message_id,preceding_twin_message_id}, twin_directives.source_conversation_id, whatsapp_messages.twin_conversation_id, brain_activity_log.user_id, pl_raw_behavioral_events.user_id, realtime_events.user_id. For reminders, the only index is a partial idx_reminders_due on (remind_at) WHERE status='pending' (20260616_create_reminders.sql:18-20) — no index covers user_id.
findings.consequence: Queries filtering/joining on these FK columns do sequential scans, and ON DELETE CASCADE from the parent (e.g. deleting a user, a twin_conversation) scans the child table. Low impact today at small data volumes, but it grows with row count and shows up first on user-deletion cascades and per-user lookups.
findings.recommendation: Add btree indexes on each flagged FK column (e.g. CREATE INDEX idx_reminders_user_id ON reminders(user_id); and covering indexes for the twin_corrections/twin_directives/whatsapp_messages FKs). Keep them minimal — single-column on the referencing column is enough to satisfy cascade and join paths.

findings.line: whole-file (prior cleanup pass) vs current advisor state
findings.title: 331 indexes reported unused by the performance advisor
findings.finding: The performance advisor returns 331 unused_index (INFO) lints. A prior cleanup migration (20260514_audit_l2_drop_unused_indexes_pass1.sql) already did one pass, so this is a known/ongoing area. Note the advisor's pg_stat figures are reset-sensitive and the shared instance also contains restaurant.* and aio.* indexes, so the raw count overstates TwinMe-specific waste.
findings.consequence: Each unused index adds write amplification (every INSERT/UPDATE maintains it) and storage/vacuum overhead without serving any read. At pre-revenue traffic the cost is modest, but it is real per-write overhead on hot tables like user_memories.
findings.recommendation: Do not bulk-drop on advisor output alone (low-traffic stats are unreliable and the instance is shared). After a representative usage window, review the TwinMe-schema subset of unused indexes, confirm against actual query plans, and drop in a reviewed pass like 20260514 did. Treat as periodic maintenance, not an urgent fix.

findings.title: Reflection-trigger threshold documented inconsistently (15 here vs 40 in CLAUDE.md)
findings.finding: The get_recent_importance_sum comment states 'Threshold is currently IMPORTANCE_THRESHOLD = 15 (~3 memories rated 5)', while the project CLAUDE.md repeatedly documents the reflection trigger at accumulated importance > 40. These are the same conceptual threshold described with two different values across the schema docs and the architecture doc.
findings.consequence: No runtime bug (the real value lives in reflectionEngine.js, not the SQL), but the contradictory documentation makes it hard to reason about reflection cadence and LLM cost — a wrong mental model here directly affects the cron cost the project is sensitive to.
findings.recommendation: Reconcile the comment to the authoritative IMPORTANCE_THRESHOLD constant in api/services/reflectionEngine.js and align CLAUDE.md to the same number, or note explicitly that recency-window sum (this RPC) and accumulated importance (engine) are different quantities if that is the intent.

findings.title: extraction_events.user_id is a bare nullable uuid with no foreign key
findings.finding: extraction_events defines 'user_id uuid' with no NOT NULL and no REFERENCES public.users(id). RLS is enabled with zero policies (intentional service-role-only telemetry, confirmed by the advisor's rls_enabled_no_policy INFO and the migration comment at lines 30-33).
findings.consequence: Orphan user_ids can accumulate (no cascade on user delete, no referential guarantee), and the telemetry census can attribute rows to non-existent or deleted users. Low impact because it is internal, low-volume telemetry, but it is an inconsistency versus the rest of the schema where user_id is a NOT NULL FK with ON DELETE CASCADE.
findings.recommendation: If user-scoped attribution matters, add 'REFERENCES public.users(id) ON DELETE SET NULL'. If it is deliberately FK-free for write-path simplicity/perf, add a comment stating so to prevent future confusion.

refuted.title: Popup connect path marks platform CONNECTED after a blind 3s timer, regardless of OAuth outcome
refuted.note:
REFUTED. The described code does not exist in the current file. The finding claims the connectUrl (popup) branch of handleConnect contains `setTimeout(() => setConnected(prev => new Set([...prev, platform.id])), 3000)` that fakes a connected state after a blind 3s timer. That line is absent — a Grep for `setTimeout|3000` across src/pages/onboarding/steps/PlatformStep.tsx returns zero matches.

The actual popup branch (PlatformStep.tsx:179-197) does the OPPOSITE of what's claimed and already implements the finding's own recommendation:
- Lines 184-188 are a comment that explicitly documents the OLD bug and its removal ("Don't fake a 'connected' state on a blind timer ... old bug: the tile flipped to a green check after 3s even if the user closed the popup or auth failed").
- Line 189 sets setConnecting(null) without marking the tile connected.
- Lines 191-195 poll the canonical server-side summary via setInterval every 2.5s calling invalidatePlatformState(queryClient), stopping after 12 polls (~30s) or when popup.closed.
- The tile only flips via connectedSet (line 257), derived from connectedProviders(platformsSummary) (line 130) — i.e. server-verified state, not an optimistic local guess.

Referenced helpers exist: invalidatePlatformState (src/hooks/usePlatformsSummary.ts:143) and connectedProviders (src/hooks/usePlatformsSummary.ts:131). Git history confirms the fix landed (ba7df9fc fix(onboarding): surface silent connect failures in cinematic PlatformStep; e66930ce single source of truth for platform state). The finding is based on a stale pre-fix snapshot.

Minor remaining note (not the claimed bug): the four RECOMMENDED platforms all use the authUrl redirect path, so the connectUrl popup branch is likely effectively dead for the current platform set — a cleanliness item at most, and it is correctly guarded either way. Adjusted severity Low to reflect this residual dead-branch observation; the High-severity false-positive-connected bug as described is not real.

strategy.gradeJustification: This is a genuinely sophisticated, well-architected codebase for a solo-founder pre-revenue product — the LLM gateway choke point, layered cost-runaway guards, centralized token refresh, HMAC-verified webhooks, AES-256-GCM token encryption, graceful context degradation, and a real CI gate (build + 200+ tests) are all above the maturity bar for this stage. The grade is held down by one Critical and a cluster of High issues that are existential rather than cosmetic: live server-side secrets (Supabase service_role key, JWT_SECRET, Google client secret) sit in git history (confirmed: commit 6d7c04b2 plus two earlier commits) and have not been rotated, which alone justifies failing a security gate. Compounding it, the database has effectively no working per-user RLS — all tenant isolation lives in the Express layer — while several PII-bearing tables (reminders, twin_calls, whatsmeow_* E2E session keys) ship with RLS fully disabled and are reachable via the anon PostgREST key. That is a one-missing-filter-from-cross-user-leak posture with no DB backstop. The architecture earns a high B on its own merits (clean acyclic service graph, thin twin-chat controller, no SQL/command injection); the security and isolation gaps drag the composite to B-. None of this is enterprise-overkill territory — these are the concrete, exploitable issues a staff engineer would block a release on.
topRisks: Live secrets in git history, unrotated: the Supabase service_role key (bypasses ALL 439 RLS policies), JWT_SECRET (forge any user's tokens → full account takeover), and GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET are recoverable from commit 6d7c04b2 and two earlier commits. Anyone with a clone, fork, or CI cache has full read/write to every user's memories, financial data, OAuth tokens, and PII. This is the single highest-impact finding and the user's own rule mandates rotation, not just deletion.

No database-level tenant isolation: per-user RLS is effectively non-functional (20260525 dropped the broken user_id policies), so isolation depends entirely on every Express route correctly applying .eq('user_id', userId). Multiple PII tables (reminders, twin_calls, whatsmeow_* WhatsApp E2E session keys) have RLS disabled and are exposed through the anon key shipped to every browser — a single missing filter, or just the anon key plus PostgREST, leaks data across the whole user base.

Background pipeline does not scale and degrades silently: observation ingestion hard-caps at 3 users/run (confirmed observationIngestion.js:390) and many per-user LLM crons (meeting-prep, morning-briefing) run serial loops with no time-budget guard, so past a few dozen users the tail is silently skipped before the 60s Vercel kill. Twin freshness — the core value prop ('Memory Is Everything') — quietly rots as the user base grows, with no error surfaced.
topOpportunities: The LLM gateway is already the single choke point for all model access (84 modules import it) with circuit breaking, caching, and layered budget guards — wiring a Redis-backed embedding cache and batching the per-observation embed+importance calls through it converts the biggest cost/latency lever into a near-free win with no architectural change.

Inngest is already in the codebase (11 functions) but underused — moving the per-user ingestion and the inline-LLM crons onto it behind thin scheduler crons removes the 3-user cap, the 60s-kill silent-skip, and the post-response fire-and-forget loss in one consistent pattern, fixing three separate High/Medium findings with one architectural decision.

The serverDb repository abstraction with built-in circuit breaker already exists as the right target — adding an ESLint/CI grep that fails when api/routes/*.js calls .from() directly (120 route files currently do) freezes the layering regression and lets the data layer be centralized opportunistically rather than in a big-bang refactor.

themes.name: Secrets & tenant isolation are a single missing-control away from full compromise
themes.explanation: The most severe findings cluster here: live secrets in git history (Critical, confirmed), RLS disabled on PII tables (reminders/twin_calls/whatsmeow_*), and the app-layer-only isolation model where one forgotten .eq('user_id') is a cross-user breach with no DB backstop. The crypto and auth primitives are excellent (AES-256-GCM, bcrypt-12, HMAC webhooks, HS256-pinned JWT), which makes the gap glaring — the locks are strong but the keys are in the doormat and several doors have no lock at all.
themes.targetState: All secrets that ever touched history are rotated; a pre-commit secret scanner (gitleaks) is in CI; every PII table has RLS enabled with a service_role-only policy; and either DB-level per-user policies exist (via an auth_user_id column) or every route's user_id filter is treated as a security control with test coverage.
themes.principle: Defense in depth: a secret in source is already compromised, and app-layer isolation needs a database backstop — never rely on a single layer of correctness for data that bypasses all of it.

themes.name: Per-user background work doesn't scale and fails silently
themes.explanation: The cost discipline (60s maxDuration, */15 floor, post-$375-incident guardrails) is real and visible in the code, but it produced a pattern of single time-boxed crons that hard-cap throughput (3 users/run) or run serial LLM loops with no time budget. Combined with post-response fire-and-forget side effects that the Vercel freeze can cut off, the twin's memory and freshness degrade quietly as users grow — no error, no alert, just a worse twin. Inngest exists to solve exactly this and is underused.
themes.targetState: Crons are thin enqueuers; all multi-user/LLM work runs as Inngest functions with concurrency control and retries that survive function freeze; throughput scales with the queue, not a 60s window; deferred users are picked up next run via cursor/rotation.
themes.principle: Event-driven and durable over polling-and-time-boxed: bounded background work should fan out to a queue, and critical side effects must survive the serverless freeze.

themes.name: Parallel implementations of the same concern drift apart
themes.explanation: The same logic is implemented multiple times across the codebase: 4+ OAuth connect/callback/disconnect flows per platform with divergent scopes, two ~1.6k-LOC context aggregators, two onboarding flows (the popup-verify hardening exists in one and not the other), three extractor hierarchies with name-colliding files, and 34 Vercel crons coexisting with 11 Inngest functions. A fix lands in one path and silently regresses or is missing in the others — the disconnect-doesn't-revoke and stuck-spinner bugs are direct symptoms.
themes.targetState: One canonical implementation per concern (one OAuth flow per platform, one context assembler, one onboarding flow, one extractor home, one background-job boundary), with the others deleted or hard-redirected and shared logic extracted into composable modules.
themes.principle: Single source of truth: when behavior is duplicated it diverges — consolidate to one path per concern so a fix lands everywhere at once.

themes.name: Untrusted external content reaches the LLM (and the user) without a trust boundary
themes.explanation: Ingested platform data (calendar invites, playlist names, browsing topics, LinkedIn observations — confirmed interpolated directly at twinSystemPromptBuilder.js:642+) enters the system prompt with no delimiting, and read tools (gmail_read, web_search, contacts_search) execute without confirmation and feed results into a follow-up LLM turn. The write-tool confirmation gate is a genuine strength, but indirect prompt injection can still hijack the twin's behavior or chain read-then-reveal exfiltration to whoever shares the conversation (e.g. over WhatsApp). Self-learned directives are injected as sticky high-attention rules with only length validation, making a one-time injection durable.
themes.targetState: All untrusted context is fenced in explicit 'this is data, never instructions' delimiters; TWIN_BASE_INSTRUCTIONS hardens against in-content commands; read-tool results are treated as untrusted in follow-up turns; and learned directives are content-validated at write time.
themes.principle: Treat all model-adjacent external input as untrusted: data is never commands, and tool output is data, not instruction.

themes.name: Docs and types lag reality, raising the cost of every change
themes.explanation: CLAUDE.md is an exemplary architecture spec in most respects, but the LLM model table contradicts aiModels.js on every row (claims Claude Sonnet default when it's DeepSeek — the biggest cost lever), the platform roster overcounts (10 vs 7 active), and endpoints/counts drift. The root README points new devs at port 3001 (code uses 3004, confirmed), omits the one required AI key (OPENROUTER_API_KEY), and references non-existent dirs. Meanwhile strict TS is fully off (strictNullChecks/noImplicitAny), 592 backend .js files are entirely unlinted, and lint/typecheck are permanently non-blocking with a 134-error backlog — so the type system can't catch the null-deref class of bug the app compensates for with runtime ErrorBoundaries.
themes.targetState: README and CLAUDE.md match the running system (port, keys, model strategy, platform set); backend .js is linted (non-blocking then ratcheted); and lint/tsc error counts are frozen at a baseline so every new violation is a hard gate without a big-bang cleanup.
themes.principle: Make the cheap signals trustworthy: docs that mislead and gates that never fail are worse than none — keep them accurate and ratchet them so they catch regressions.

doneSignals: Every secret that appeared in git history (Supabase service_role key, JWT_SECRET, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET, OpenRouter/webhook secrets) is rotated and the old values are confirmed revoked; gitleaks runs in CI and blocks on new secret introduction.
get_advisors / RLS lint returns zero ERROR-level findings: reminders, twin_calls, and all whatsmeow_* tables have RLS enabled with service_role-only policies, verified against a fresh DB.

Observation ingestion and the inline-LLM per-user crons run via Inngest (or a shared runUsersBatched helper with a TIME_BUDGET_MS early-exit) such that no user is silently skipped at 50+ users; MAX_USERS_PER_RUN is a fallback, not the throughput governor.
A CI grep fails the build when api/routes/*.js calls .from() directly, and the count of direct-DB route files is frozen at or below today's baseline (no new violations).
ESLint covers api/**/*.js (no-undef, no-unused-vars, no-unreachable) and runs in CI; lint+tsc error counts are recorded as a baseline and CI fails if either exceeds it.

goalTrackingService extractMetricFromPlatformData and extractMetricFromMemories have direct unit tests covering each metric type, the focus_time math, METRIC_BOUNDS rejection, and each regex branch; the empty-content streaming path has a regression test.

README boots a fresh machine correctly (port 3004, OPENROUTER_API_KEY documented, no dead links), and the CLAUDE.md LLM-strategy table and platform roster match aiModels.js and the live VALID_DEMO_PLATFORMS set.

Disconnect revokes the OAuth grant at the provider (best-effort, logged) before deleting the row, via a single shared disconnect helper; untrusted platform content is fenced with explicit data-not-instructions delimiters in the system prompt.

milestones.name: Milestone 0 — Safety net
milestones.goal: Stop the bleeding and make regressions visible before changing behavior: rotate compromised secrets, prevent recurrence, and put cheap guardrails on the gaps that have no backstop today.

tasks.title: Rotate all secrets that ever touched git history
tasks.description: Treat the Supabase service_role key, JWT_SECRET, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET, and any OpenRouter/webhook secrets from the committed .env.* files (commit 6d7c04b2 + dab978bd + 70eabebd, confirmed) as compromised. Regenerate each, update Vercel env, and confirm old values are revoked. JWT_SECRET rotation forces a global re-login — acceptable for a pre-revenue user base.
tasks.dependencies: None — do this first, before any other work
tasks.acceptance: All four secret classes regenerated and old values confirmed invalid (e.g. old service_role key returns 401). Global re-login confirmed working.
tasks.risk: Medium — JWT rotation logs everyone out; service_role rotation must be deployed atomically with Vercel env or the API 500s. Coordinate the swap.

tasks.title: Add gitleaks pre-commit + CI secret scan
tasks.description: Add a gitleaks (or equivalent) scan as a pre-commit hook and a CI step so no secret can be committed again. The repo already had a 6-file env leak; this is the forcing function.
tasks.acceptance: A test commit containing a fake AWS/Supabase-shaped key is blocked by the hook and by CI.

tasks.title: Enable RLS on PII tables with service_role-only policies
tasks.description: Add ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY + 'FOR ALL TO service_role' policies to reminders (confirmed no RLS at 20260616), twin_calls, and every whatsmeow_* table (E2E session keys). Backend uses service_role/BYPASSRLS so this is deny-by-default-once-enabled and low-risk. Verify the bridge connects with service_role before enabling.
tasks.acceptance: get_advisors returns zero ERROR lints for these tables; anon-key SELECT on reminders/twin_calls/whatsmeow_* returns no rows; bridge + reminders/calls still function.
tasks.risk: Medium — if any of these tables is read by a client using the anon key, enabling RLS will break it; verify access path first.

tasks.title: Freeze layering and lint/type regressions with CI baselines
tasks.description: Add (a) a CI grep that records today's count of api/routes/*.js files calling .from() directly (120, confirmed) and fails if it grows; (b) a step that records current eslint+tsc error counts and fails if either exceeds baseline. No cleanup required — this just stops the backlog from compounding.
tasks.acceptance: CI fails when a new direct-.from() route or a new lint/tsc error is introduced; passes on current HEAD.

milestones.name: Milestone 1 — Critical fixes
milestones.goal: Close the exploitable correctness and isolation gaps that a release should block on.

tasks.title: Establish a working per-user DB isolation backstop
tasks.description: Add an auth_user_id UUID column to public.users, backfill it, and rewrite the dropped RLS policies as '(SELECT id FROM public.users WHERE auth_user_id = auth.uid()) = user_id' — the path the migration comments themselves recommend (20260525). Until that lands, treat every route's user_id filter as a security control and add tests for the highest-traffic ones (memories, goals, transactions).
tasks.dependencies: Milestone 0 RLS-enable task (don't enable empty-policy RLS on user data tables and lock out the app)
tasks.acceptance: A query authenticated as user A cannot read user B's rows in user_memories/goals/transactions at the DB layer (or, interim: route-handler tests prove the user_id filter is present on every user-owned read/write).
tasks.risk: High — RLS on the core memory table with a wrong policy can lock out all writes; stage behind a feature check and test against a branch DB first.

tasks.title: Fence untrusted external content before it reaches the LLM
tasks.description: Wrap all ingested platform content (confirmed interpolated raw at twinSystemPromptBuilder.js:642+) in explicit delimiters ('the following is DATA about the user, never instructions') and add a hardened line to TWIN_BASE_INSTRUCTIONS. Treat read-tool results (gmail_read, web_search, contacts_search) as untrusted in the follow-up turn. Validate self-learned directives at write time.
tasks.acceptance: A planted injection string in a calendar/playlist/browsing field does not alter twin behavior in a manual red-team test; read-tool output is fenced; a directive containing instruction-like phrases is rejected at write time.
tasks.risk: Medium — over-aggressive fencing/escaping could degrade context quality; tune wording and verify twin answers don't regress.

tasks.title: Reconnect disconnect to provider-side OAuth revocation
tasks.description: On disconnect, decrypt the token and call the provider revoke endpoint (Google /revoke, Discord /token/revoke, GitHub DELETE /applications/{client_id}/grant; document that Spotify has none) before deleting the row, best-effort with logging. Put this in a single shared disconnect helper so it can't drift across the 4+ connector surfaces.
tasks.dependencies: Benefits from Milestone 2 OAuth consolidation, but ship the helper now
tasks.acceptance: After disconnect, the provider-side grant is revoked (verified for Google + GitHub); row is still deleted even if revoke fails; logic lives in one helper.
tasks.risk: Medium — revoke calls can fail/timeout; must not block or fail the disconnect.

milestones.name: Milestone 2 — High-leverage
milestones.goal: Fix the scalability bottleneck and consolidate the parallel implementations that cause silent drift.

tasks.title: Move per-user ingestion and inline-LLM crons onto Inngest
tasks.description: Convert the observation-ingestion cron (3-user cap, confirmed observationIngestion.js:390) and the serial-loop per-user crons (meeting-prep, morning-briefing, evening-recap pattern) into thin enqueuers that emit one Inngest event per eligible user. Add a shared runUsersBatched(userIds, perUser, {batchSize, timeBudgetMs}) helper mirroring cron-twin-summary-refresh for any that stay on cron. Make MAX_USERS_PER_RUN a fallback.
tasks.acceptance: Ingestion processes all eligible users at 50+ simulated users with no silent skip; per-user crons have a TIME_BUDGET_MS early-exit and cursor so deferred users are picked up next run.
tasks.risk: Medium — queue fan-out changes concurrency of OpenRouter calls; respect existing budget guards and concurrency limits.

tasks.title: Batch the embedding + importance LLM calls in ingestion
tasks.description: Replace the per-observation serial embed+importance loop (observationIngestion.js:545-583, the highest-frequency LLM call) with batchGenerateEmbeddings (already cache-aware) and a single batched importance rating call (or default platform_data to the importance floor and skip the rater). Back the embedding cache with Redis keyed by content hash (getCacheKey already exists) so it survives serverless invocations.
